source.title: Dissecting the evolvability landscape of the CalB active site toward aromatic substrates

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper addresses the challenge of engineering CalB lipase to improve activity toward bulky aromatic substrates (methyl cinnamate and methyl salicylate) due to its poor native recognition. The goal is to develop an iterative semi-rational design (ISRD) strategy that combines computational docking, residue interaction network (RIN) analysis, and iterative saturation mutagenesis (ISM) to create smaller, smarter mutant libraries, overcoming limitations of high-throughput screening for transesterification reactions.

section_gists.heading: Dissecting the evolvability landscape of the CalB active site toward aromatic substrates
section_gists.gist: Introduces the ISRD approach combining virtual docking, RIN analysis, and ISM to engineer CalB for improved activity on bulky aromatic substrates. Highlights the challenge of screening large mutant libraries and the need for semi-rational design. CalB is chosen as a model due to its industrial relevance, stability, and limited activity on bulky substrates. The strategy aims to identify hot spots, perform saturation mutagenesis, and iteratively improve variants.
process_signals: Problem: CalB has poor activity on bulky aromatic substrates.
Constraint: High-throughput screening for transesterification is difficult.
Hypothesis: Combining computational design with targeted mutagenesis can efficiently improve activity.
Approach: ISRD merges structure-guided design with empirical data.
failures_or_limits: CalB natively has low activity against bulky substrates.
Typical directed evolution strategies are limited by screening capabilities.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Describes optimization of CalB expression in E. coli Rosetta (DE3) and development of a colorimetric transesterification assay using vinyl substrates and MBTH. The ISRD strategy is outlined: (A) identify hot spots via docking and RIN, (B) screen mutant libraries, (C) model best variants for next round. RIN analysis of 136 previous mutations showed >80% of beneficial mutations had low betweenness centrality, guiding selection of residues less likely to disrupt network. Docking identified 11 active-site positions for saturation. First round libraries (G1) screened 60-66 colonies each; variants D134S, D134T, T138N, T138A, T138G, T138S showed improved methyl cinnamate production. Second round (G2) combined T138G with other positions; T138G-D134S and T138G-V190A showed further improvement. Third round (G3) added mutations to T138G-V190A; T138G-V190A-I189V and T138G-V190A-Q157L showed highest activity. Overall, 12 improved mutants found by screening only 0.308% of theoretical library.
process_signals: Expression optimization: Rosetta (DE3) in SB medium, 0.1 mM IPTG, 16°C.
Screening assay: vinyl cinnamate/salicylate, MBTH detection of acetaldehyde.
WT CalB selected as starting template over L278P variant.
RIN analysis: low betweenness centrality correlates with beneficial mutations.
Docking identified 11 positions; libraries designed with 95% coverage.
Iterative rounds: G1 single mutants, G2 double, G3 triple.
Best mutants: T138G-V190A-I189V and T138G-V190A-Q157L.
failures_or_limits: Some variants showed no improvement or reduced activity (e.g., T138G-I189G).
Screening limited to 60-66 colonies per library, may miss rare beneficial mutations.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Discusses the motivation: lack of proper high-throughput screening for transesterification. ISRD focuses on smaller, smarter libraries. Only 0.308% of total sequence space screened, yet 12 improved mutants found. Highlights that mutations were concentrated in active site, where most effective. Notes that WT CalB has poor activity on bulky substrates, but engineered variants show significant improvement. The approach is validated as a proof of concept. Limitations include not exploring all active-site positions and potential epistatic effects. The iterative nature allowed accumulation of beneficial mutations.
process_signals: Motivation: no reliable HTS for transesterification.
Strategy: target active-site cavity to maximize effect.
Efficiency: 0.308% of theoretical library screened.
Success: 12 improved mutants over 3 generations.
Limitation: only 11/23 positions explored.
Future: could explore remaining positions or combine with other methods.
failures_or_limits: Not all active-site positions were explored.
Screening assay may not capture all activity nuances.

section_gists.heading: Experimental Procedures
section_gists.gist: Details methods: codon-optimized CalB gene in pET22b+ with C-terminal His-tag. Tested 5 E. coli strains; Rosetta (DE3) best. Expression in SB medium, 0.1 mM IPTG, 16°C, 48h. Activity confirmed by esterification assay on agar plates with oleic acid/1-decanol and rhodamine B. Enzyme lyophilized from phosphate buffer for organic solvent assays. Virtual docking: substrate-imprinted docking with Molegro Virtual Docker, using PDB 1TCA, ligands methyl cinnamate and methyl salicylate. Two rounds of docking with side-chain minimization. RIN analysis: betweenness centrality calculated for 136 previous mutations. Site-saturation mutagenesis: NNK primers, libraries transformed into Rosetta (DE3), 60-66 colonies screened per library. Screening: 96-well plate assay with vinyl substrates, MBTH, absorbance at 595 nm.
process_signals: Expression optimization: Rosetta (DE3) selected from 5 strains.
Lyophilization for organic solvent tolerance.
Docking: induced fit simulation, two rounds.
RIN: centrality measure to predict mutational tolerance.
Library design: NNK saturation at selected positions.
Screening: colorimetric assay in 96-well format.
methods: Esterification plate assay with rhodamine B
failures_or_limits: Library screening limited to 60-66 colonies, may not achieve full coverage despite 95% theoretical.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research evolved from the problem of poor CalB activity on bulky aromatics and lack of HTS for transesterification.
Hypothesis: Combining computational hot spot identification with iterative saturation mutagenesis can efficiently navigate sequence space.
RIN analysis provided a novel filter to select mutable residues with low network disruption.
Iterative rounds allowed accumulation of beneficial mutations, with each round informed by modeling of previous best variants.
The approach successfully demonstrated that small, focused libraries can yield significant improvements, validating ISRD as a general strategy.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Were all beneficial mutations additive, or were there antagonistic epistatic effects?
How transferable is the ISRD approach to other enzymes or substrate classes?
What is the structural basis for improved activity in the best mutants?
Could further improvements be achieved by exploring remaining active-site positions or combining with random mutagenesis?
How do the engineered variants perform under true industrial conditions (e.g., higher substrate concentrations, continuous flow)?

events.summary: The functional analysis and identification of active variants among millions of mutational possibilities is not trivial.
A combinatorial semi-rational approach can help design smaller and smarter mutant libraries.
The ISRD strategy combines virtual docking, iterative saturation mutagenesis, and RIN analysis.
Optimized CalB overexpression in E. coli Rosetta (DE3) using SB medium, 0.1 mM IPTG, 16°C.
Developed a liquid medium transesterification screening assay using vinyl substrates and MBTH detection.
RIN analysis of 136 previous CalB mutations showed >80% of beneficial mutations had low betweenness centrality.
Selected WT CalB as starting template over L278P variant due to similar synthetic performance.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk introduces the problem, hypothesis, overall design strategy, and initial experimental steps including expression optimization and assay development.

events.summary: Selection of active-site mutational hot spots by combining virtual docking energy ranking and RIN betweenness centrality.
Design of first-generation (G1) site-saturation mutagenesis libraries at positions 40, 134, 138, 154, 157, and 189 based on favorable energy contributions.
Expansion of mutational exploration to additional positions (39, 104, 190, 278, 285) in later generations (G2 and G3) using new docking templates.
Screening of G1 libraries identified 6 variants with increased methyl cinnamate transesterification, notably T138G, T138A, T138S, T138N showing up to 4-fold improvement.
Combination of G1 and G2 mutations (T138G with V190A or D134S) yielded double mutants with nearly 6-fold improvement in methyl cinnamate production.
For methyl salicylate, a double mutant V154L-L278A showed 3.6-fold increase in activity, despite WT CalB having negligible activity.
RIN analysis revealed the catalytic cavity is divided into three main residue modules, guiding systematic mutational exploration.
G3 library design combined positive mutations from G1 and G2 to test additive or synergistic effects, using templates T138G-V190A and T138G-D134S.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed design rationale, experimental results from G1-G3, and analysis of mutational trajectories.

events.summary: RIN analysis shows that each evolutionary trajectory is preceded by module reorganization of residues forming the catalytic pocket.
Both double mutants T138G-V190A and V154L-L278A stabilize substrate aromatic rings, allowing proximal alignment of the carbonyl moiety 2–4 Å from catalytic S105.
Combining beneficial G2 substitutions D134S and V190A with T138G had deleterious effects on CalB performance towards methyl cinnamate synthesis.
RIN analysis shows D134S substitution in T138G-V190A perturbs the core protein network, altering 10 residue interactions outside the active-site cavity.
Normal Mode Analysis suggests D134S substitution initiates large-scale rigidification of functional motions in CalB, explaining deleterious effects.
Only 11 out of 23 active-site cavity positions were explored, and screening 360 variants per trajectory represents only 0.308% of total sequence space.
Screening 60–66 transformants per library over 3 generations yielded 12 mutants with improved activity, validating the ISRD approach.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed analysis of RIN changes, structural modeling, NMA, and evolutionary trace, along with discussion of limitations and validation of the ISRD strategy.

events.summary: RIN analysis suggests two additional hydrogen bonding interactions may help stabilize enzyme-substrate complex formation in both evolutionary trajectories (T138G-V190A and V154L-L278A).
W104 plays a determinant role for coordination and orientation of catalytic triad residues, namely through formation of new H-bonding interactions with H224, and for substrate stabilization.
Replacement at position W104 leads to drastic reduction of the beneficial effects conferred by mutations T138G-D134S.
Synthetic activity improvements rely on proper substrate accommodation in the active-site cavity rather than important increases in enzyme-substrate affinity.
Vinyl salicylate activity correlates with a decrease in active-site cavity volume, which still provides proper accommodation of the substrate.
ISRD approach uncovered newly productive CalB variants, acting as an efficient method to quickly engineer enzyme properties with minimal screening effort.
Mutations T40A and T40V were not observed in any evolutionary pathways, probably due to the importance of T40 as an essential enzyme-substrate complex contributor.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains discussion and analysis of results, including RIN analysis insights, structural interpretations, and validation of the ISRD approach.

events.summary: Virtual docking involved two rounds of optimization with side-chain energy minimization.
methods_or_tools: guided Differential Evolution (DE) hybrid search algorithm

events.summary: Individual saturation mutagenesis libraries were designed for each selected active-site position.
The ISM strategy fixed best mutations from one round as templates for subsequent libraries.
Screening effort was estimated to require 66 clones per library for 95% coverage of 22 variants.
Transesterification screening used vinyl substrates and MBTH detection of acetaldehyde.
methods_or_tools: Tecan Infinite M1000 PRO microplate reader

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed methods for virtual docking, library design, screening, and RIN validation, which are core to the ISRD strategy.
This chunk contains only references, acknowledgements, author contributions, competing interests, and license information. No research evolution events are described.

evidence_bank.quote: the functional analysis and identification of active variants among such high numbers of mutational possibilities is not a trivial task
we describe a combinatorial semi-rational approach to partly overcome this challenge and help design smaller and smarter mutant libraries

By adapting a liquid medium transesterification assay in organic solvent conditions with a combination of virtual docking, iterative saturation mutagenesis, and residue interaction network (RIN) analysis
the most efficient bacterial CalB producer was found to be E. coli Rosetta (DE3) grown on SB medium (0.1 mM IPTG induction, 16°C)
we adapted and standardized a liquid medium CalB synthetic activity screening assay using vinyl analogs to mimic cinnamic and salicylic acid compound recognition
We found that more than 80% of previous beneficial mutations that either increase CalB activity or improve protein stability exhibit low values of betweenness centrality measure
Due to similar synthetic performance relative to the previously reported variant CalB-L278P, we selected WT CalB as a proper starting template for all future protein engineering steps

Identification and selection of active-site mutational 'hot spots' by convergence of two approaches. First, identification of residue positions that stabilize the ligand-enzyme complex is achieved by virtual docking, after which involvement of those positions in the maintenance of structure and topology of the active-site cavity is performed by a Residue Interaction Network (RIN) analysis

amino acid replacements exhibiting high energy contributions to binary complex stabilization and low values of betweenness centrality measure were selected as promising positional targets for mutagenesis

For the first generation of mutants (G1), we designed 6 distinct site-saturation mutagenesis libraries at CalB positions 40, 134, 138, 154, 157, and 189, which were then individually screened against both substrate analogs. These semi-randomized positions were selected according to favorable energy contributions with respect to enzyme-substrate complex stabilization
mutational exploration of the active-site cavity was expanded to CalB positions 39, 104, 190, 278, and 285 for subsequent library generations (G2 and G3)

the most active methyl cinnamate G1 variants were observed for randomized positions T138 and D134 (Fig. 3A), where 6 variants showed increased transesterification activity relative to WT. The most efficient G1 variants are T138G, T138A, T138S, and T138N, displaying up to 4-fold increase in methyl cinnamate production relative to WT CalB

The addition of G2-selected mutations (V190A and D134S) to the G1-T138G template allowed selection of double mutants displaying production yields nearly 6-fold that of the WT conversion rate for the same compound

we nevertheless selected a double mutant (V154L-L278A) exhibiting 3.6-fold increase in catalytic activity relative to WT CalB. Noteworthy is the fact that WT CalB shows negligible synthetic activity against vinyl salicylate (<0.5% conversion)
Our RIN analysis shows that the catalytic cavity of CalB is divided into three main residue modules, which describe clustering interactions defined by the Linkland algorithm

For G3 library design, we opted to combine positive mutations selected in G1 and G2 to investigate additive or synergistic behavior on catalytic activity. As a result, two initial G1-G2 templates were used for further active-site exploration: T138G-V190A and T138G-D134S

Changes in RINs under selective pressure shows that each evolutionary trajectory (vinyl cinnamate or vinyl salicylate) is preceded by module reorganization of the residues forming the catalytic pocket.
Both double mutants T138G-V190A and V154L-L278A favor stabilization of substrate aromatic rings, allowing proximal alignment of the carbonyl moiety 2–4 Å away from the catalytic S105 (dashed line).
active G2 substitutions (D134S and V190A) in the context of the favorable G1 single mutation T138G had deleterious effects on the performance of CalB towards methyl cinnamate synthesis

the effects of such substitution propagate outside the active-site cavity, perturbing the core of the protein network (Fig. S4C). More precisely, D134S reorganizes the core network and alters a set of 10 residue interactions
Normal Mode Analysis (NMA) of both double and triple variants suggests that the D134S substitution can initiate large-scale rigidification of the functional motions experienced by CalB
exploration of 11 out of the 23 active-site cavity positions in CalB was performed

Our RIN analysis suggests that two additional hydrogen bonding interactions may help stabilize enzyme-substrate complex formation in both evolutionary trajectories (T138G-V190A and V154L-L278A).

Our results also suggest that W104 plays a determinant role for coordination and orientation of catalytic triad residues, namely through formation of new H-bonding interactions with H224, but also for substrate stabilization in both complexes
replacement at position W104 leads to drastic reduction of the beneficial effects conferred by mutations T138G-D134S (Fig. 3C).
This observation suggests that synthetic activity improvements rely on proper substrate accommodation in the active-site cavity rather than important increases in enzyme-substrate affinity.
Interestingly, vinyl salicylate activity correlates with a decrease in active-site cavity volume (Table SII).
These results illustrate that the ISRD approach uncovered newly productive CalB variants, acting as an efficient method to quickly engineer enzyme properties with minimal screening effort.

although mutations T40A and T40V were suggested as potentially important enantioselective contributors, we did not observe these substitutions in any of our evolutionary pathways, probably due to the importance of T40 as an essential enzyme-substrate complex contributor

The process involves 2 rounds of virtual docking optimization, where the most energetically favorable structure of the first round is used as a template for side-chain energy minimization in the catalytic pocket of the enzyme.
Based on virtual docking predictions, an individual saturation mutagenesis library was designed for each selected position in the active site of CalB.

This strategy was coherent with the Iterative Saturation Mutagenesis (ISM) approach (Fig. 1, center), where the best mutations selected in one round are fixed and used as starting template for subsequent library design.
our screening effort required sequencing of 66 clones per library to explore 95% diversity
Substrates were used on a 3:1 ratio of methanol (Sigma) and vinyl cinnamate (or vinyl salicylate) (Aldrich), two analogs of the target substrates cinnamic acid and salicylic acid.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Engineering CalB lipase to improve activity toward bulky aromatic substrates (methyl cinnamate and methyl salicylate) due to poor native recognition, while overcoming limitations of high-throughput screening for transesterification.

canonical_events.summary: CalB has poor activity on bulky aromatic substrates and high-throughput screening for transesterification is difficult.
A combinatorial semi-rational approach (ISRD) combining virtual docking, iterative saturation mutagenesis, and RIN analysis can design smaller, smarter mutant libraries.
RIN analysis of 136 previous CalB mutations showed >80% of beneficial mutations had low betweenness centrality, guiding selection of mutable residues.
Selected WT CalB as starting template over L278P variant due to similar synthetic performance.
Optimized CalB overexpression in E. coli Rosetta (DE3) using SB medium, 0.1 mM IPTG, 16°C.
Developed a liquid medium transesterification screening assay using vinyl substrates and MBTH detection.
Selection of active-site mutational hot spots by combining virtual docking energy ranking and RIN betweenness centrality.
Design of first-generation (G1) site-saturation mutagenesis libraries at positions 40, 134, 138, 154, 157, and 189.
Screening of G1 libraries identified 6 variants with increased methyl cinnamate transesterification, notably T138G, T138A, T138S, T138N showing up to 4-fold improvement.
Expansion of mutational exploration to additional positions (39, 104, 190, 278, 285) in later generations (G2 and G3) using new docking templates.
Combination of G1 and G2 mutations (T138G with V190A or D134S) yielded double mutants with nearly 6-fold improvement in methyl cinnamate production.
For methyl salicylate, a double mutant V154L-L278A showed 3.6-fold increase in activity, despite WT CalB having negligible activity.
G3 library design combined positive mutations from G1 and G2 to test additive or synergistic effects, using templates T138G-V190A and T138G-D134S.
Combining beneficial G2 substitutions D134S and V190A with T138G had deleterious effects on CalB performance towards methyl cinnamate synthesis.
RIN analysis shows D134S substitution in T138G-V190A perturbs the core protein network, altering 10 residue interactions outside the active-site cavity.
Normal Mode Analysis suggests D134S substitution initiates large-scale rigidification of functional motions in CalB, explaining deleterious effects.
RIN analysis revealed the catalytic cavity is divided into three main residue modules, guiding systematic mutational exploration.
Both double mutants T138G-V190A and V154L-L278A stabilize substrate aromatic rings, allowing proximal alignment of the carbonyl moiety 2–4 Å from catalytic S105.

edges.reason: The problem of poor activity and screening difficulty motivates the hypothesis of a semi-rational approach.
The ISRD hypothesis leads to RIN analysis to identify mutable residues.
RIN analysis informs the selection of WT CalB as the starting template.
Starting template requires optimized expression conditions.
Optimized expression enables development of the screening assay.
Screening assay is needed to test hot spot selections.
Hot spot selection leads to design of G1 libraries.
G1 library design leads to screening and identification of improved variants.
G1 results motivate expansion to additional positions for G2/G3.
Expanded positions allow combination of mutations yielding double mutants.
Parallel trajectory for methyl salicylate yields V154L-L278A.
Double mutants serve as templates for G3 library design.
G3 design leads to testing combinations, revealing deleterious effects.
Deleterious effects prompt RIN analysis to understand perturbation.
RIN perturbation leads to Normal Mode Analysis for dynamic explanation.
RIN analysis also reveals modular organization of the cavity.
Structural modeling of double mutants explains stabilization.

must_cover_points: Problem of poor CalB activity on bulky aromatics and screening difficulty.
ISRD hypothesis combining docking, ISM, and RIN.
RIN analysis showing low betweenness centrality correlates with beneficial mutations.
Selection of WT CalB as starting template.
Hot spot selection by docking and RIN.
G1 library design and screening yielding T138G variants with 4-fold improvement.
G2 combination yielding T138G-V190A and T138G-D134S with 6-fold improvement.
Methyl salicylate trajectory yielding V154L-L278A with 3.6-fold improvement.
G3 design and deleterious effect of combining D134S and V190A.
Validation: 12 improved mutants from screening 0.308% of sequence space.
